---
title: "Staffing the Feasibility Phase"
subtitle: "Civic Data Access · roles, the one real hire, and what the verdict must say before you scale"
author: "Strategy / project-management working document"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
---

# How to read this document

This is the companion to the feasibility-test design. That document
defined **what experiment de-risks the company**. This one answers a
narrower, dangerous-if-gotten-wrong question:

> **Who do you hire — and at what level, and when — to run the
> auto-mapping proposer testing phase?**

The danger is not under-hiring. It is **over-hiring the wrong skill**:
staffing the testing phase as though it were primarily a data-science
problem, when it is overwhelmingly an experiment-design and
project-management problem with a small, sharp data-science core.

The single sentence to keep in view:

> The phasing of *permanent* data expertise must be **downstream of the
> verdict**, because the verdict tells you which kind of company you are
> hiring for.

---

# The trap in the question "what expertise per stage?"

The intuitive move is to assign a seniority level of data scientist to
each stage of the test. That framing is wrong, and acting on it produces
a bad hire.

Re-read what the feasibility test actually requires: freezing corpora,
quarantining the answer key, agreeing thresholds in writing, recruiting
a *non-engineer* test subject, classifying interventions, producing a
signed-off verdict. That is mostly **discipline and protocol** labor.
The genuine data-science core is real but narrow and time-boxed.

So the productive framing is not "expertise per stage." It is:

> **What distinct roles does this experiment require, which ones must be
> the same person, which absolutely cannot be, and which is the only
> real hire?**

---

# The four roles the experiment actually requires

The roles are *deliberately mismatched in seniority*. Hiring uniformly
senior data people across all of them would **weaken** the experiment.

## Role 1 — The Proposer Builder *(the one real hire)*

- **Skill:** genuine data/ML depth — schema inference, fuzzy entity
  resolution, schema-era detection, draft-config emission.
- **Scope:** bounded. Build a proposer good enough to *test*, not to
  ship. Over-engineering here before the verdict is the exact
  "build the satisfying part" trap.
- **Hard constraint:** must **not** also be the person who scores
  whether it passed. Builder-grades-own-homework is the primary way
  feasibility tests lie.
- **Employment form:** **contractor / bounded statement of work, not a
  permanent senior hire.** Before a Green verdict the proposer is a
  research instrument, not a product. A permanent senior data-science
  hire here is premature commitment — scaling the team before the
  experiment that justifies the team.

## Role 2 — The Experiment Owner *(this is you — do not hire it out)*

- **Skill:** protocol integrity and discipline, not data science.
- **Owns:** inputs frozen, answer key quarantined, thresholds committed
  *in writing before the run*, verdict mapped honestly to the table.
- **Why you:** this is the most important seat in the testing phase and
  the one founders most often leave structurally empty because it is
  less satisfying than building. If it is not held with authority, the
  test has no teeth. You have already demonstrated this instinct;
  occupy the seat formally.

## Role 3 — The Ground-Truth Steward *(also you — likely not hireable)*

- **Skill:** deep domain judgment of the TEA corpus + data sense.
- **Owns:** confirming the answer key is correct and expressible in the
  config schema; adjudicating the step-6 classification —
  *was this intervention mechanical or institutional judgment?*
- **Why not a hire:** that adjudication is the crux result of the entire
  experiment, and it rests on 29 years of hand normalization that is
  not a transferable résumé credential. A new hire would be guessing at
  distinctions you know in your bones. Trying to delegate this
  outsources the one judgment the company's verdict depends on.

## Role 4 — The Non-Engineer Test Subject *(deliberately NOT an expert)*

- **Profile:** a genuine finance / records-analyst type. **Not** a
  developer. **Not** you.
- **Purpose:** the NEC metric (can a non-engineer finish the proposer's
  draft?) is only valid if the measured person is genuinely
  non-technical. Staffing this with someone too skilled silently
  invalidates the most important secondary metric.
- **Note:** this is not a hire to *staff* the project — it is a person
  (hired or borrowed) to *be the measured subject*. The instinct to put
  your best person on the test destroys the test here.

---

# Role-to-person map

| Role | Who | Employment form | Can it be shared? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 · Proposer Builder | External specialist | **Bounded contract / SOW** | Must NOT also score (Role 2/3) |
| 2 · Experiment Owner | **You** | Founder | May share with Role 3 |
| 3 · Ground-Truth Steward | **You** | Founder | May share with Role 2 |
| 4 · Non-Engineer Subject | Borrowed/hired analyst | Short engagement | Must NOT be you or a developer |

The firewall that matters most: **Role 1 is structurally separated from
Roles 2 and 3.** The builder builds; the founder (owner + steward)
judges. They are never the same person.

---

# The one real hire, specified: the Proposer Builder contract

This is the only genuine hiring decision the testing phase forces. Spec
it tightly.

## What they must be able to do

- Ingest heterogeneous spreadsheets (multi-tab, multi-era, inconsistent
  headers) and emit a **declarative draft mapping config** — not
  per-corpus bespoke code.
- Implement: field identification, entity canonicalization, schema-era
  detection, authority/version resolution, cross-year linkage
  (the five decision types from the feasibility test).
- Attach a **confidence signal** to each emitted decision (this feeds
  the Failure-Honesty metric — non-negotiable, not a nice-to-have).
- Work to a config **schema you control**, not invent their own format.

## Bounded statement of work (shape, not legalese)

- **Deliverable:** a proposer that runs *cold* against a supplied corpus
  and emits a scored-against-ground-truth draft config, plus
  per-decision confidence.
- **Explicitly out of scope:** the chatbot, the chart UI, the retrieval
  engine, production hardening. (Those are post-verdict. Including them
  now is the trap.)
- **Duration:** fixed. A research instrument, not a product build.
- **Acceptance:** the proposer runs reproducibly and emits the artifacts
  the scoring needs — *not* "the proposer achieves X% accuracy."
  Whether it hits the threshold is the **experiment's** finding, judged
  by Roles 2/3 — never an acceptance criterion the builder is paid to
  meet (paying the builder on the accuracy number recreates
  grade-your-own-homework through the contract).

## The explicit firewall clause

- The builder supplies the instrument and its raw outputs.
- The builder does **not** have access to the quarantined answer key.
- The builder does **not** score DAR/NEC/Failure-Honesty.
- Scoring and verdict are owned solely by Roles 2/3 (you).

---

# When permanent data hires happen — and why only then

Permanent data expertise is **deferred until after the verdict**,
because the verdict determines *which company you are staffing*:

| Verdict | What it means you're hiring for | Permanent data team implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Green** (DAR ≥ 80%, NEC ≥ 90%, Honesty ≥ 95%) | Turnkey software company | Lean data team; invest in proposer + template library as core IP |
| **Yellow-Template** (DAR 50–79%, NEC ≥ 90%) | Template-driven software company | Hire for *template library* curation; different skill mix than turnkey |
| **Yellow-Watch** (DAR ≥ 65%, NEC < 90%) | Higher-touch onboarding company | Hire onboarding/solutions specialists, not just modelers; price for it |
| **Red** (DAR < 50% or Honesty < 90%) | Not yet a Model B company | Do not hire permanently. Re-design proposer or reconsider model |

Hiring a permanent senior data team *before* the verdict commits payroll
to a company whose shape is still unknown. Each verdict needs a
*different* permanent team. That is the whole reason the one real hire
is a **bounded contract**, not a permanent seat.

---

# The full process, in one sequence

1. **Occupy Roles 2 & 3 yourself, formally.** Owner + Steward are you.
2. **Freeze raw TEA inputs and the hand-normalized answer key;
   quarantine the key** from anyone who will tune the proposer.
3. **Confirm the answer key is expressible in the config schema.**
   If it is not, the schema is wrong — record that as an early finding
   before any build.
4. **Write the four metric thresholds down** (DAR, NEC,
   Failure-Honesty, Time-to-First-Trustworthy-Config) and sign them
   before anything runs.
5. **Scope and contract Role 1** (Proposer Builder) on a bounded SOW
   with the firewall clause. Builder gets raw inputs and the config
   schema — never the answer key, never the scoring.
6. **Acquire the two secondary corpora:** one non-TEA municipal
   (generalization), one deliberately hostile (failure-mode probe).
7. **Recruit Role 4** — a genuine non-engineer analyst — for the NEC
   test. Not you. Not a developer.
8. **Run the proposer cold** against all three corpora.
9. **Roles 2/3 score** DAR, NEC, Failure-Honesty,
   Time-to-First-Trustworthy-Config. Classify every intervention as
   *mechanical* vs. *institutional judgment*.
10. **Map results to the verdict table. Record the verdict and the
    numbers.** This signed verdict — not a working product — is the
    deliverable of the entire phase.
11. **Only now** decide the permanent data team, sized and shaped to
    the verdict that came back.
12. **Only after a Green or re-scoped Yellow:** begin engine/demo build.

---

# The two failure modes this document exists to prevent

1. **Over-hiring the wrong skill.** Staffing the test with uniformly
   senior data scientists feels rigorous but actually weakens the
   experiment: it removes the non-engineer signal (Role 4) and invites
   builder-grades-own-homework (Role 1 ≠ Roles 2/3). The roles are
   mismatched in seniority *on purpose*.

2. **Premature permanent commitment.** Hiring a permanent data team
   before the verdict scales an organization whose shape is still
   unknown. Each verdict implies a *different* permanent team. The one
   real hire in this phase is a bounded contract; everything permanent
   waits for the number.

The discipline is the same one running through the whole project: build
and staff the part that **proves** the company before the part that
**polishes** it — and let the verdict, not enthusiasm, authorize the
next dollar of payroll.
